items.text: Chapter Three
Nomenclature and Common Names of Tuta absoluta
Introduction

The pest Tuta absoluta is known by several scientific, international, Arabic, and local names. This multiplicity results from the transfer of the insect among several genera throughout its taxonomic history, its spread across different geographical and linguistic regions, and the diversity of plant parts attacked by its larvae.

Some names have focused on the mines formed by the larvae inside tomato leaves, whereas others have emphasized the insect’s ability to bore into stems, growing points, and fruits. Certain names have also been associated with the insect’s region of origin in South America.

In Egypt, the pest is primarily known as “Tuta,” while some growers refer to it as “the weevil” or “tomato weevil.” It is also sometimes confused with the pest commonly known in the field as “leaf miner,” although the latter name usually refers to leaf-mining flies belonging to the genus Liriomyza, not to Tuta absoluta.

Understanding the pest’s names is therefore directly related to correct diagnosis, because Tuta absoluta and leaf-mining flies differ in taxonomy, morphology, life cycle, damage sites, monitoring methods, and control programs.
Scientific Name
The modern taxonomic scientific name of the insect is:
Phthorimaea absoluta Meyrick, 1917
The name by which the pest has become widely known worldwide during recent decades is:
Tuta absoluta (Meyrick, 1917)
Both names refer to the same insect species, not to two different pests.

The name Tuta absoluta has been the most widely used in scientific research, extension publications, integrated pest management programs, pheromone traps, and biological control products. A recent taxonomic study subsequently reassigned the insect to the genus Phthorimaea, making its modern taxonomic name Phthorimaea absoluta. [1, 2]
For this reason, the insect appears in references and databases under two forms:
The name “Tuta absoluta” remains the best-known name among agricultural engineers, growers, and professionals involved in tomato production.
Interpretation of the Scientific Name
The scientific name consists of two parts:
Genus Name
Phthorimaea
It begins with a capital letter when written in Latin script.
Species Name
absoluta
It begins with a lowercase letter.
The genus and species names are written in italics:
Phthorimaea absoluta
The name of the scientist who described the species and the year of description are not italicized:
Phthorimaea absoluta Meyrick, 1917
The author’s name appears in parentheses when the former name is written:
Tuta absoluta (Meyrick, 1917)
The parentheses indicate that the insect was not originally described within the genus Tuta, but was transferred to it after its original description.
After the full name has been written for the first time, it may be abbreviated as:
P. absoluta
Or, in references using the widely recognized name:
T. absoluta
History of Scientific Nomenclature
The British entomologist Edward Meyrick described the insect in 1917 based on specimens collected from Peru in South America.
At the time of its original description, it was named:
Phthorimaea absoluta Meyrick, 1917
It was subsequently reclassified several times as understanding of the relationships among genera in the tribe Gnorimoschemini within the family Gelechiidae changed.
Historically, the insect appeared under the following names:

The name Tuta absoluta became widely used after the insect was reassigned to the genus Tuta in 1994. A morphological taxonomic analysis published in 2021 subsequently showed that the species belongs to a group that includes Phthorimaea operculella, the type species of the genus Phthorimaea. Accordingly, the insect was returned to its original genus. [1, 2]
Former Scientific Names and Synonyms
All the following names refer to the same pest:

rows: Most widely recognized and commonly used name

items.text: These names do not indicate the existence of different species; rather, they represent the successive stages through which the classification of the species has passed.
The former names are important when reviewing older studies, because some research on pest biology, distribution, and control was published before the name Tuta absoluta became widely adopted.
International Pest Code
In the database of the European and Mediterranean Plant Protection Organization, the insect is assigned the code:
EPPO Code: GNORAB
The first part of this code is derived from the former name:
Gnorimoschema absoluta
The code has remained unchanged despite the transfer of the insect among different genera.
This code makes it possible to link records of the same species within international databases regardless of changes in the scientific name.
The pest appears in plant-protection databases under:
Common International Names
Tomato leafminer
The literal Arabic translation of this name is:
Tomato leaf miner
The name Tomato leafminer is used in many international references and publications concerning Tuta absoluta.
The name describes the ability of the larvae to enter between the two leaf layers and feed on internal tissues, resulting in the formation of mined blotches or tunnels.
However, the term Leafminer is not a taxonomic name specific to one species. It is a general description applied to any insect whose larvae feed within leaf tissues.
Different insect species may therefore carry the name Leafminer while belonging to widely separated orders and families.
South American tomato leafminer
This means:
South American tomato leaf miner
Or:
Tomato leaf miner of South America
The name links:

items: The pest’s region of origin in South America.
The larva’s mining behavior within tomato leaves.

items.text: The descriptor South American reduces confusion with other leaf-mining species.
Tomato leafminer moth
This means:
Leaf-mining tomato moth
The name combines the form of the adult insect with the nature of larval damage.
The adult insect is a small moth, whereas the larva is the stage that enters plant tissues and forms mines.
South American tomato moth
This means:
South American tomato moth
It indicates that the insect:

items: Is a small moth belonging to the order Lepidoptera.
Is economically associated with tomato crops.

items.text: This name is more accurate than the word “butterfly” when describing the adult insect, because the insect belongs to the group of small moths.
Tomato borer
This means:
Tomato borer
This name reflects the fact that the damage caused by Tuta absoluta is not restricted to leaves.
The larvae can attack:
The name Tomato borer describes the boring behavior within the plant more broadly than the name Tomato leafminer.
Tomato pinworm
The name Tomato pinworm appears in some references associated with Tuta absoluta, but this name is also used primarily for another pest:
Keiferia lycopersicella
Both pests belong to the family Gelechiidae, and the larvae of both can damage tomato leaves and fruits, but they are two different species. [4]
Therefore, the name Tomato pinworm alone does not establish the identity of the pest unless the scientific name is also stated.
South American tomato pinworm
This means:
South American tomato pinworm
The phrase South American is added to distinguish Tuta absoluta from other pests known as Tomato pinworm.
Nevertheless, the names:
Remain clearer when referring to this pest.
Common Names in Several Languages
The pest has spread across large areas of the world and has acquired different local names.
These names revolve around three principal elements:

items: 1. Tomato as the principal economic host.
2. Mining or boring within tissues as the larval feeding behavior.
3. The moth as the form of the adult insect.

items.text: Arabic Name
The pest appears in Arabic references under several names, including:
The name:
> Tuta absoluta — Tomato leaf and fruit borer
Is among the names that most clearly express both the identity of the pest and the nature of its damage.
The term “Tuta absoluta” identifies the insect species, while the description “Tomato leaf and fruit borer” clarifies that the damage is not restricted to leaves alone.
Tuta absoluta
The name “Tuta absoluta” is the phonetic rendering of the widely recognized scientific name:
Tuta absoluta
This name has the advantage of directly identifying the pest without confusing it with other mining insects.
It is widely used in:
Tomato Leaf and Fruit Borer
This name describes the principal sites of damage caused by the larvae.
They often begin feeding inside leaves and may subsequently attack:
This name reflects the economic severity of the pest because fruit infestation causes direct loss of marketable or processable yield.
Tomato Leaf Miner
This name is the literal translation of:
Tomato leafminer
It describes the mines or mined blotches formed by the larvae within leaves.
However, this name may cause confusion in Egyptian field usage because the terms “leaf miner” or “leaf-miner” are commonly used to refer to leaf-mining flies of the genus Liriomyza.
Furthermore, describing Tuta absoluta only as a leaf miner does not include its damage to stems, growing points, and fruits.
Tomato Miner

This name describes the larva’s behavior within the tomato plant in general, but by itself it does not identify the pest species because tomatoes are attacked by several insects that may bore or mine inside leaves, stems, or fruits.
The specific name should therefore always be linked to Tuta absoluta:
The tomato miner, Tuta absoluta.
South American Tomato Moth
This name describes both the adult insect and its region of origin.
The adult is a small moth belonging to the family Gelechiidae, while South America is its native region before its spread into Europe, Africa, Asia, and the Middle East.
Names of the Pest in Egypt
Tuta
“Tuta” is the most widely used name among growers and agricultural engineers in Egypt.
It is an abbreviation of the name:
Tuta absoluta
Common field expressions include:
The name “Tuta” refers to the pest in all of its stages, although each stage has a different function:

items: The egg represents the beginning of the generation.
The pupa is the transformation stage.
The adult moth is responsible for mating, dispersal, and egg laying.

items.text: Tuta Moth
The expression “Tuta butterfly” is used to refer to the winged adult insect.
The more accurate entomological term is:
Tuta moth
Because the adult insect is small and belongs to the moth group.
The adult moth does not mine into leaves or fruits; it mates and lays eggs. Direct damage begins after the eggs hatch and the larvae emerge.
Tuta Worm
Growers use the expression “Tuta worm” for the stage found inside leaves or fruits.
The scientific name for this stage is:
Tuta larva
It is the stage responsible for:

items: Boring into growing points and stems.
Opening entry points for secondary rots.

items.text: Tomato Miner
The name “tomato miner” is used in Egyptian extension and technical reports and describes the feeding behavior of larvae within plant tissues.
These larvae differ from larvae that feed openly on the leaf surface because they spend a large part of their feeding period protected within:
The Weevil or Tomato Weevil
Some growers refer to Tuta absoluta as:
Particularly when they observe holes or larvae inside the fruits.
This is a popular name that does not reflect the true taxonomic classification of the pest.
Tuta absoluta belongs to:
Order Lepidoptera
The order that includes moths and butterflies.
True weevils belong to:
Order Coleoptera
The order that includes beetles and weevils.
Accordingly, Tuta absoluta is not a true weevil, even if this name is used for it in some agricultural areas.
Difference Between Tuta and Leaf Miner in Tomato
Meaning of the Term Leaf Miner
The term:
Leaf miner
Literally means:
An insect that produces mines in leaves or a leaf-mining insect.
It is not the scientific name of a single insect species, but a description of the way in which a larva feeds inside leaf tissue.
The mining insect may be:

items: An insect belonging to another order.

items.text: Therefore, the presence of a mine within a leaf is not sufficient by itself to identify the insect species.
What Is Meant by Leaf Miner in Egyptian Fields?
When the name “leaf miner” is used in Egyptian tomato fields, it usually refers to:
Leaf-mining flies — Liriomyza spp.
Known species within this genus include:
These insects belong to:
The adult is a small fly, usually black and yellow, while the larva lacks visible legs and resembles a small white or yellow maggot.
The larvae of Liriomyza form thin, winding, or serpentine mines within leaves. [3]
Tuta absoluta Is Not a Leaf-Mining Fly
Tuta absoluta belongs to:
The adult insect is a small moth, not a fly.
The larva is a lepidopteran larva with:

items: A body whose color changes with age.

items.text: The larvae usually form broad, blotch-like, and irregular mines within leaves, and dark frass may be present inside the mine. They can also move from leaves to stems, growing points, and fruits. [5]
Comparison Between Tuta and Liriomyza Leaf Miner

rows: Lepidopteran larva with a head and legs
Accumulates within parts of the mine
Appears as a line or dots within the mine track
Leaves, stems, growing points, and fruits
Not a typical form of damage

items.text: Appearance of Tuta Mines
Tuta infestation may begin as a narrow mine and then expand as the larva grows, becoming:

items: An irregular white or gray area.
Dry brown tissue in old infestation.

items.text: Several mines may merge and damage a large portion of the leaflet.
The larva can leave one mine and move to another leaf, stem, or fruit. Empty mines may therefore be found alongside mines containing live larvae.
Appearance of Liriomyza Mines
The mines formed by Liriomyza flies begin as thin lines and gradually widen as the larva grows.
They are commonly characterized as:

items: Containing a trail of frass within the mine.

items.text: Some mines may widen or overlap under severe infestation, but they usually retain the characteristic winding appearance. [3]
Difference Between Tuta and Other Similar Pests
Tuta and Tomato Fruitworm
Tomato fruitworm is commonly associated with the species:
Helicoverpa armigera
Its larva is larger than the larva of Tuta absoluta, feeds more openly, and produces relatively large holes in fruits.
The Tuta larva is smaller, usually begins within leaves, and then enters the fruit through a small opening that may be located near the calyx or elsewhere on the fruit surface.
Tuta and Potato Tuber Moth
The potato tuber moth is:
Phthorimaea operculella
Whereas Tuta absoluta is:
Phthorimaea absoluta
They are two different species within the same genus.
The potato tuber moth is primarily associated with potatoes and potato tubers, whereas tomato is the principal economic host of Tuta absoluta.
There is some resemblance between the adults of the two species, so accurate identification requires examination of diagnostic characters or the use of species-specific pheromone traps.
Tuta and Tomato Pinworm
The tomato pinworm of North America is known as:
Keiferia lycopersicella
Its larvae may form blotch mines in leaves and subsequently attack fruits.
This insect differs from Tuta absoluta, despite the fact that both belong to the family Gelechiidae and cause similar types of damage. [4]
Accurate Field Terminology

rows: Tuta absoluta in all of its life stages
The mining stage responsible for damage
Eggs laid by females on the plant
A mined blotch or area within the leaf
Larval holes or tunnels inside fruits
An inaccurate popular name for Tuta
Sex pheromone specifically attracting male Tuta moths
A trap containing a pheromone specific to the pest

items.text: Importance of Correct Naming in Diagnosis and Control
Accurate naming is not merely a linguistic issue; it is directly linked to the integrated pest management program.
Confusing Tuta with leaf-mining flies may lead to:

items: Using a pheromone unrelated to the pest present.
Selecting an unsuitable insecticide or application timing.
Confusing a lepidopteran larva with a fly larva.
Neglecting inspection of fruits and stems.
Applying a control program targeting a different insect.
Incorrectly evaluating the effectiveness of natural enemies.

items.text: When mines are found in tomato leaves, diagnosis should include examination of:

items: Presence of the larva inside the mine.
Shape of the larval head and legs.
Presence of infestation in growing points or stems.

items.text: Mine shape alone does not identify the pest in every case, particularly during the early stages of infestation or when several mines overlap.
Chapter Summary
The modern taxonomic scientific name of Tuta absoluta is:
Phthorimaea absoluta Meyrick, 1917
The name most widely recognized worldwide and in the field is:
Tuta absoluta (Meyrick, 1917)
Its international common names include:
Its Arabic names include:
In Egypt, it is mainly known as:

items: Weevil, which is a popular but taxonomically incorrect name.

items.text: Tuta absoluta does not represent “leaf miner” in the common Egyptian field sense. The term leaf miner usually refers to leaf-mining flies of the genus Liriomyza, which are small flies belonging to the order Diptera.
Tuta absoluta, in contrast, is a moth belonging to the order Lepidoptera. Its larvae form blotch mines in leaves and also bore into growing points, stems, and fruits.
The principal field distinction is therefore:
> Tuta: a moth whose larvae bore into leaves, growing points, stems, and fruits.
> Leaf miner: a fly whose larvae primarily form winding mines inside leaves.
References
